speaker: Rob Moore
words: Walter Benscoter and Norla Callison have been farmers all their lives. They hopped over clods behind a ... and years later rode over the same dirt in a tractor. Their perspective is that of men who have stayed in one place, done the same job, and watched the place and the job change. On this tape, they reminisce with each other about the old one-room schools, weather and crop changes, Halloween tricks, hell raising, shivaree, sneak thieves and prohibition.

words: I was going to town till we were all dead. I wasn't with the kids, I wasn't even there. But you heard it. I don't think anybody would mind these days. That one was the right call. A shitty trick. That one right on. Well, it seems like most of the tricks that people played in those days really were a lot more fun and a lot less mean. They were more to get people to laugh than to hurt people. Well, I tell you, I think the kids in them days, by and large, really, I think they really enjoyed themselves much. Once in a while they would ski a little bit, but they're mostly skated, isn't it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Of course, they didn't. And then it ended up when you go over to somebody's house and the party broke up, you know. You go up to the house and they have an ostrich stew.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was this more when your little kids or when you were growing up?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, we were kids going to school and grades over there.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, the grades?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't think the real little kids that were turned out to be. First and second grade they were living in all of it. Of course, we had kids here in them days. I went over here one of my 45s, and dad went over to the 60s. You could get a bus like that and you could have a pretty nice time escaping or coaching. And I went up to the house and had an oyster feast. Oyster stew. Do you remember those old barrel steaks we used to go coasting on, just take a barrel steak and put a block on it and put a seed on it. That's the block, about like a milking stew. And try setting on that fish. You couldn't control it for one bit, you know. I tell you, you've got quite a thrill of size.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And you used to have sleigh ride partners, do you remember?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, toboggans, you remember when we used to ride over here on ... school?That ride over here on the big tree is that steep coal run over there. Straight off. It used to drift the snow, drift up the top of the hill, 6 .85. You'd be a jump off there, high on your head. Yeah, high on your head, you know, or high on your head. Come down over that thing and boy, I'm going to tell you when you hit the cross. You'd be a jump off there high on your head. Yeah, that old boy would crack like a rifle.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And you had to be careful to take your breath away. Yeah, you can do my job. Going that fast.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When you started to get older, how did the courtship, you know, when you were starting to get older and dating and things like that, how did those things work in the country in the early days? What kind of things did you do then?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I think you just took your girl walking. Use the tank for me.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They used to go to church, you know, and you'd probably walk down home from church. Sometimes they'd go to the bar, they'd get hungry. That's right. They didn't hurry, they'd go home, you know, they had lots of time. And then the sleigh ride parties, you'd take a big old bobsled and put some straw in there. If you're lucky enough to have a foot warmer, you're going to keep your feet warm, you know, and the blankets, the horse blankets, you know. Horses, you know, with the sleigh bells across the hangers, you know, and the skinnter, he was the driver. The rest of them back in there, of course, bundled up, trying to keep some freezing. And of course, the boys couldn't let the girls get cold. I remember one time, we went to Troy. I forget how many there was of us, but I remember Nellie Maevers' law. She was over the rest of us. And coming home, I just had old Troyian tricks. So there weren't too many of us, but there wasn't enough for a sleigh ride anyway. I can't remember whether one could dance or what one went to. But anyway, coming home, I got sleepy. I tied up the line and crawled down onto the covers and went to sleep. And Nellie Maevers said, we met two rigs in Bethel Gorge up there. And the team turned out for them, but I didn't know it. I had to do nothing about it. She came to work. She came to work. And her home was, and it turned out there didn't go by.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: One time, Harry and Ernie and I went to Randall Plateau then. She'd been off to Randall Plateau. One of the coldest places I've ever allowed to be. That's north, that's why.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Northwest?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. Well, we went up there, Harry and I, and Ernie had one pretty good horse, and Harry had it pretty good. Ernie and I, I come down, Ernie and I went over, and Harry had a friend. We harnessed up the team, and Harry and Horace and Ernie and Horace, and I went around and opened up to a dance. Man, it was bull zero. I was coming up near a freezing dance. I went up there and danced in one of them sweet gals, you know. We used to have some fine times there. Well, that's a lot of some rasp we're bringing into the moonshine, too, that's what I was cropping. And then I asked Horace, she was sleeping in that bed with him out over there. My guy had got time for us to come home, and we weren't home. Boy, she's spending all the night. It was broad daylight, and she was getting over it. And she kept looking. She just knew that something had happened to them guys, and she said it was broad daylight, and said, I looked out, and here come Ernie and I, and I and he and the old man, walking home and we left there, and Horace was glad to hear me come in there broad daylight, coming across the field out of the Oh, that's what you'd call young and irresponsible.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When a man decided on a woman he thought he might like to marry, would the courtship take quite a while?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That's a good answer. That's a good answer. I tell you, an art number didn't get married very young. I think that was 28.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: How old were you, Bob?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Going on 32.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Remember the harvest balls you used to have down here at the temple?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Boy, it's had a harvest ball ever fall. Boy, that was some dance. They decorate that all up with pumpkins and corn stalks or cornshocks of corn, you know. Man Brothers, you remember them, they come up here and they were a good orchestra. They were, well, after they outgrewed Lewis and here, they played big time. They played Seattle, San Francisco, plays a lot of that. And the big hotels there, they were tops. Yeah, Man Brothers played there, and then there was another, after Man Brothers left here and went into the big circuit. Why, then there was coffins. That's a good part of that. You know, the coffins used to come up here. We used to have the best times down there, you know, and the full harvest was all done. They had a big harvest ball. And, well, I didn't bowl out. Look at that now, the way the crowds used to get in there. They had some wonderful times there. To one of that old temple that came in, I saw that floor covered with people. That's right. They condemned it. Well, I didn't break down, I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Still condemned, I guess, isn't it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I think, you know, they got some steel posts in there. That's pretty good now. I've been hearing something about, after people got married, their neighbors would throw a shivaree for them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did that happen much up here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, it never failed. All of that shivaree, you know. You just figure on buying the creeps. Shotguns in the works.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would a shivaree be? What would people do in a shivaree?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Just make all the cock-eyed noise you can make. That was about all the shit. What it really should have been was music, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was it called?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I'm just kind of serenade. I'm just serenaded. It was just a bunch of noise.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that the night they got married or anything?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, the first night they brought them home. Then they got a honeymoon, you know, by the... The couple always knew they were coming. And they'd have candy bars, you know, and apples and different things for treats, you know, and coffee. And they'd move in, you know, the crowd would be in there. They always wanted to wait until they got in bed if they could. Wait, wait for them. They'd sneak in, you know, and that noise that turned loose outside, you know, and there was always some rasp or two in every shotgun. You get right beside that. I'll shoot right up in the airline cap and tin cans and stuff.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Remember that little horse fiddle you used to have?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That little rig they turned to the crank, they called a horse fiddle. You know... It's a call. They're running on a call black cat and that always a noise, you said. I think that's still coming. I think it's a careless old shit. It is. I think it is. Well, you know, you want to hang on to that. That'd tell you to be something. That made the office noise. And they turned that thing into a crank and there was a thing running on a call. They jumped from one call to the other. Oh, brother, talk about an old one. They'd get them up, you know, by the... Out of bed, you know, and they'd invite them in, you know, and the noise is this. That was the main idea, to get them up, you know, and then they'd sit around, you know, and visit and eat candy bars and apples. Just have a nice evening. I didn't remember if my memory isn't good anymore. But Kyle Anderson told me the other day, you know, when we were shivaree, I don't know whether you came or not. I imagine. The Woody Boy was there and Kyle, I can't remember who all. But anyway, he knew that we weren't prepared for him. So he came and told me. You better get that man. So I went to town, got some cigars, and I was in the car. And he got prepared for the shivaree, and it took place, so I... Got the treats. And we had ice cream. I don't know. And then days, the people would have it. In some ways, I think they're happier, happier now. People got so much know that they should be happy. But they just got so much that... I know one thing, we used to visit a lot now. Oh, have Pinnacle parties. They have parties, you know. People would go to church, and the neighbor would invite them home for dinner. We would invite somebody home to our place, you know, and people would visit. And they had parties, you know. Literary, you remember, you used to have literary. My God, now, you know, you never see your neighbor. You wave at him across the... Across the door, across the fence on your own one part, and he's on the other. Wave at him going to come in town. But actually, as far as going over to a neighbor to visit, and spend the day and eat dinner with him, he was pretty unheard of. It's just people don't... They'll get in the car and drive 50 or 100 miles somewhere to visit somebody. That went out when the car came in. That's right. But when they had horses and the old buggy or the hack, you didn't get too far away from home.

speaker: Rob Moore

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, sure. Yeah, you get together and you dance in the kitchen. It's just boys are just left in overalls, you know. One guy maybe played a squeeze box and another in the fiddle, you know. That's why it's called kitchen sweats, you know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Does everybody get tickets in there and sweats?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, they just... They was in that place and they'd have to take you in there and you'd get parts, you know. And they'd get up a sweats, you know. That's pretty good action stuff. But they had a lot of fun, I don't know. They enjoyed... It didn't take much them days to make people happy. People got so now, you know, that it takes an awful lot to make them happy. You could have a good time at a kitchen-sweating fair overalls. In fact, that's about all they ever wore. The man used to wore... He wore a fair overall, a clean shirt, you know. And no admission, I don't think, was ever charged. Maybe they might have took past the hat to play the fiddler and the guy playing the... The squeeze bottle. Remember, they used to have dance tools at San Beggins over the... Oh yeah. Oh, I can see that old fiddler. You're old friend. I can see that old fiddler sitting up there, yeah. Oh, just the kid. That mother used to go... I can... He's just as plain as he is, I can see that. He's a pretty good size haul. Yeah. That was a lot bigger than the kitchen. They should really come on and find a dancer, you know. And if the old building still stands, I think it does. Yeah, it's still there. Don't look too bad shape either.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And barn dancers, they used to have barn dancers. Remember, they had to quite a series of barn dancers down there, where Steve Remberly is. Remember that... They turned out to be quite a... Quite a dance business.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He was the one that had the ... paralysis, you know?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: But Stewart lives in... I believe he lives at... You know, he lives at Hayden Lake. It's just about... Edgy's for Edgy Cordellagney.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Chantilly?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No, Stewart. Oh, Stewart. Stewart, I think it's Hayden Lake, just Edgy Cordellagney. Yeah, somebody told me during the last year that they saw Stewart. When I was about Stewart's boy, I...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Dean? Dean?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, he was a pretty nice kid, I think. He was a nice kid. I don't know where Dean's at. Last I knew, Dean, he was in California. But he's a handsome asshole. He used to hold beans for me and dad. He was a good kid. I suppose he was a fine looking kid. About six foot two inches tall and just a small kid. Would there be big parties and things too, after...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Like after they finished thrashing out one farm or something? Would there be a party there to kind of celebrate that?

words: They just moved to another farm.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How many thrashing rigs were there, like on this end of the ridge? What was that? How many thrashing rigs were there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: One year, there was... Maybe more than one year, there was only one.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Old Chris Mars, you remember?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: The fall of the grain got wet. He thrashed to hold rigs that year, but the next year, that was when the company machine was. Well, then before the company machine, you remember George Flamm used to... Yeah. He used to thrash up here. George Flamm. He thrashed for us a number of years. Yeah. And one of you may use the thrash. Yeah. And just bought. But, one of you used to go over that on the Genesee Company too. Yeah, he was pulling over there. I get bigger run over there. He thrashed a time or two for Dad. Yeah. But George Flamm thrashed for us several years. And then way back along, as Indian says, a long time ago, Martin Thomas. Yeah, Martin used to thrash the lower end of the ridge. Yeah, he used to thrash for us. I remember more than once. Yeah. He thrashed for us up there and by and gone. No, he never whistled. He always said that he had a thrash machine, not a whistler machine.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The whistle was to stop work?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, any kind of signal or anything, he'd always whistle. It stopped, you know, the whistle for noon and they'd choose a little bit for the thrash, you know. And Martin, there were some guys, you know, we were on the road, see somebody pull the whistle too, Dad, you know. Martin said he had a thrash machine. So they were thrashing there at home and the engine was setting just about where Bob's Corral is. And the separate was going that throttle and that stack bottom there. And Dad was doing the milking. And I just went shaper, I think I milked about one cow or two. And we was doing the milking. And the guys went to the house, he'd back me. And we got through milking. Dad says, we're going to hear that whistle. Dad got up and pulled the whistle back and tied it.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Tied it down. And I got him Martin. The mother said Martin is he was Mother's brother. Yeah, I know. And then anybody but my daddy killed him. Martin cleaned up on his brother-in-law. And the mother said Martin sat there, that big old mustache is, you know, and cut it to the inside. He just sneezed and snored, but he never got up his breakfast. But she said the minute he got up, he was got up and he went out. Long word, that whistle.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, he lowered the steam, didn't he?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He just tied it out. Pulled her back and wrapped the wire on the water. Let her go. Yeah, he didn't kill him, it was a bit anybody but his brother-in-law.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, most of the time there were two machines on the ridge. Yeah, I don't know, there was only one year. One time there were three. One time there were three. There was Chris Meyer and the company machine and then that little company ridge that Perry and... Oh yeah, I remember that. That time there were three thrashing machines. That was right at the end of the thrashing. That was the next thing we were called by. That was just before he came in.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would it work that somebody would just go out and buy a thrashing machine and then rent it out to help pay it off or what?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, they just bought it and then they went out and cut what they made it out of. I don't think anybody ever wanted to butt this here with a thrashing machine. No people. That's what you mean. People here was good about paying the thrash bills. Some thrash amends would go broke if they didn't pay that bill. People here... Well, broken or already they couldn't collect it. People here paid the thrash bills. Right before the combine there were three thrash machines. There were two company rigs.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was company rigs worth it? You bought in shares on the machine?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: There were six of them in our rig. It started out with Byron Davison and Wade Keaton, Walt Bingham, George, and Frank Roberts and Matt.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: When Wade quit he sold iron. Oh, it's iron. Iron haven. And...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Byard... Bingham sold to...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Amos, remember when the ring had to go to army?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He sold to Amos.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And Byard... I don't know what he sold or what the ring was, but anyway, Cliff, come on. He wound up in the rig. But after the original six was the ones I named for. Dad and Frank and George, they were in it all the time from the start to the finish. I bought that old machine the fall of 16. I'll never forget that. Granddad and I went to Elk River fishing over the old Maxwell. And there was an old Altman Taylor tractor, a 27-inch Altman Taylor. Pretty good old machine that was.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: An old international tractor that burnt distance, you remember?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And the pistons that were down this way. Yeah. George run the set there and lastly run the engine. They got that machine and while we was at Elk River fishing, they were the old Maxwell's over there a couple days.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: When we come home, the old man guy, remember, had that place down there on the grade?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And he had a good piece of wheat, about 50 years ago. And he prevailed on the boys to thrash his wheat while he was coming up to grade, bringing it home. And they pulled in there and thrashed as the first thrash in the dump.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Was that piece on the old guy place?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And they come on up on hill and they left the rest of town. That was the first bucking, too. That's right. That's what introduced buck wheat to this company. Yeah, you know, each company man pranked a bottle wagon in a tank the whole week. That was a big season. Yeah. Each company owner had a bottle wagon in a hole in the wheat, and they created it, you know. Each man, there were six of them, and each had a tank and four and some of them used five horses on the wagon. And they'd just run on this mountain, hold it both, you know. And at the end of the year, they got the weights, said they had a grand jury settling up some night in the Georgia Davidson. And they figured out what their expenses was and how much it was going to cost. And then each man was assessed that much, according to the amount of wheat he had. The amount of wheat he had, a man that had 2 ,000 mugs of wheat would pay more than a man that had 12-wonder books. And he figured out on that basis that he was as fair as could be. What their expenses was, again, what amounts that he had pranked. And then the difference in the bottle wagon, why that was just trade work back and forth.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: A fellow who had a small crop, which he'd come out longer on the holly. He'd get paid for hollies. He'd haul more for the other guy than they'd haul for him, say. Say one guy had 15-wonder books a week, and the neighbor over here had 3 ,000. Well, when they traded, he'd get a little back. And on the bottle wagon hauling, they paid so much for a wagon and team to all bums. Well, I'm going to say that it was $4, $5 a day. I remember when you got $5, that was an awful price. Yeah, $4 a day. You worked, well, say I worked for Walt three day, whole bums, one three day. Well, he hauled for me two and a half days. I owe him half a day, see. That's the way to figure it out. It wasn't hard to figure out. I think $4 was the going wages for a man, a team, and a wonder wagon. I remember when he got up to find all. Lester Crocker was pitching that year. He thought that was awful. Find all for a pitcher. He'd pitch one of them on your final all over again. That was after things started to pick up. That's right. That was war days. That's right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: In the days before this company machine came in, and people traded work around Thrashen time, did people ever take advantage of that? Or was everybody pretty straight with their neighbors, or how did it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Some people you'd be difficult to settle up with. I don't ever remember having trouble with anybody.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: How many people here were all honest?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You know, something... We didn't have any. Up until the time that old Perry Huddleston moved over here out the corner, nobody ever locked anything. There was nothing. They never locked up. You didn't have to worry about anything. Well, you didn't worry, because nobody took anything. But then it started. They started coming in, and stealing started to take place. About that time, you know, everyone was like, you'd lose a ham, or a side of your tube of bacon. Things started to move around. But the people lived here. The old original settlers, you didn't have to worry about anything. They were just as honest as they did. You didn't have to worry about anything. I've heard stories about how, like, up in the Princeton country, when, like, say a ham would disappear or something, and the people would know who it was, they'd go and take care of themselves.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did it work around here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, we never did take care of old Perry, but he should have been dead. He's terrible. We knew how. We knew where it was going.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You remember they found them hams over there, pulling out wood pollen, pulling their own, what, a jenny's wing?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No, I don't know.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Did you hear about that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No. Oh, yeah, it was about the time that they bruised meat, and I forget who it was when they were there. They cut them with it, and they found a hammer toothed, cashed away in a pile where they'd pile the limb wood up where they'd been making wood, you know.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They found it next spring, it was rotten.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Pretty good idea where, who put it there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. You got pretty tight, you see, pretty hot. You just let them. Well, how did people try to enforce the law, or to enforce, you know, the... Oh, hell, I didn't remember. We didn't have a sheriff.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What would people do then in case something went wrong?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: In case there was some sort of fighter, you know... We never had any trouble with it. Oh, I never had any trouble with it. We never had any trouble with it.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, you remember that time old Perry got into Wade Teens' cellar under the house where George lived?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, but, you know, he had the trap set for him. Mmm, I don't know, you know. Wade and him was going to Moscow.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Decoration day wasn't it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No, he was in preparation day, and he rigged up a shotgun.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, and by the way, who was it that was piling out there with a walking pile on that face there that Dick wanted?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No, I don't think there's anybody... I was planting beans. Somebody made it to you and they heard the gun walk. I heard the gun walk, but I thought the wind was blowing like the devil, and I thought a board that blew off... And slapped. You know, and come down flat. Yeah, well... I never even looked. I just kept blowing up the beans. Anyway, when Wade never come home that afternoon, or when they come home, for the first thing, I... Wade... He thought I was going to go down and see how his trap... trap worked. He opened the door and I stood Perry. He couldn't get out, see.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
